zotero.abstractNote: Time pressure is a ubiquitous feature of decision-making in the real world, yet its effect on exploration remains poorly understood. We investigate how time limits influence exploration in a multi-armed bandit task. Participants were given either a generous or a restrictive time limit to make a sequence of choices. We find that time pressure leads to more exploitative behavior, characterized by a reduction in the exploration of uncertain options. This effect is driven by a shift in the underlying decision strategy, with participants under time pressure relying more on simple heuristics rather than more complex, information-seeking strategies. Our results suggest that time pressure acts as a constraint on the cognitive resources available for exploration, leading to a more conservative and less adaptive decision-making process.
zotero.conferenceName: 41st Annual Conference of the Cognitive Science Society
